speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. This time we're focusing on Git authentication, which sounds technical, but the core idea is simple: when GitHub needs proof that it's really you, you need a secure way to provide that proof.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that usually becomes relevant when someone is working in VS Code or the command line and suddenly tries to push, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If you're only browsing public repositories on GitHub.com, reading issues, or even cloning public repositories, you often won't need authentication at all. But the moment you want to push commits, clone a private repository, or access organization repositories with restricted permissions, GitHub needs to authenticate you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not a universal first-day task for every learner.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If someone is working entirely in the GitHub web interface, or using GitHub Desktop in a way that already handles sign-in for them, they may be able to skip most of this. But if they're using VS Code with Git at the command line and want to push to a fork, this becomes important.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we go further, I want to call out the accessibility guidance built into this reference, because that matters for our audience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. For screen reader users, this appendix is structured around two main paths: Personal Access Tokens, often called PATs, and SSH keys. The steps are presented as numbered sequences, which is helpful because screen readers announce position, like "1 of 8," as you move through them. And the troubleshooting section is organized by recognizable error messages, so you can jump to the one that matches what you heard or saw.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for low vision users, the practical takeaway is that command examples are meant to be readable in code blocks, but it's still wise to zoom in, use your preferred theme, and move into a terminal or editor view that respects your settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Also, the security warnings are visually emphasized in the original material, especially the "do not" guidance. And for sighted users, the reference is designed so you can compare methods first, then follow the one you choose, and later scan troubleshooting by error message.

speaker: ALEX
text: There are two main authentication methods here: Personal Access Tokens and SSH keys.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the plain-language version first. What is a PAT?

speaker: ALEX
text: A Personal Access Token is a long password-like string generated on GitHub. When Git asks for credentials over HTTPS, you use your GitHub username as the username, and the token instead of your normal account password.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it behaves like a substitute password, but it's really a separate credential with its own permissions and expiration.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And that leads to the pros and cons. PATs work on all operating systems. They're relatively easy to set up, especially for screen reader users, because most of the setup happens in the GitHub web interface rather than through terminal configuration. They can be scoped to specific permissions, and if one is compromised, you can revoke it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The downsides are that you have to store it securely, and it expires.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. You need a safe place to keep it, and eventually you'll need to generate a new one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now SSH keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: SSH uses public-key cryptography. You generate a pair of keys on your computer: a private key, which stays on your machine, and a public key, which you upload to GitHub. When GitHub challenges you, your private key proves your identity without exposing the private key itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the appeal is that once it's working, it feels almost invisible.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's one of the biggest advantages. SSH keys typically work automatically once set up, they avoid repeated password prompts, they're generally more secure than tokens in everyday use, and they don't expire by default.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But setup is more complex.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It usually requires terminal commands and a bit more configuration, which can make it less accessible for some learners, especially those who are still getting comfortable with command-line workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So for this workshop, which path is recommended?

speaker: ALEX
text: The recommended path is creating a Personal Access Token. Not because SSH is bad, but because PAT setup is more approachable in a workshop context and more accessible through the GitHub web interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk through that carefully.

speaker: ALEX
text: Step one is generating the token. You go to GitHub's token settings page at github dot com slash settings slash tokens. In the left sidebar, choose Tokens classic. Then activate Generate new token and select Generate new token classic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then you're asked to describe it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In the Note field, give it a meaningful label, something like Workshop Laptop Token, so you'll recognize it later. Then choose an expiration. For temporary workshop use, 30 days or 60 days is recommended.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What permissions should learners select?

speaker: ALEX
text: At minimum, the reference recommends the repo scope, which covers repository access, including public repo work and private repositories. If you'll be working with GitHub Actions workflows, also include workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then generate it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Scroll down, activate Generate token, and then comes the most important warning: copy the token immediately. GitHub will show it once. If you navigate away without saving it, you generally can't reveal that same token again. You'd have to generate a new one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a big moment for screen reader users too.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. The token appears as a long string in a text field. A practical workflow is select all with Control plus A, copy with Control plus C, and paste it immediately into a secure location.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which brings us to step two: storage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Best option: a password manager, such as 1Password, Bitwarden, or another trusted manager. Also acceptable: an encrypted secure note provided by your operating system. A plain text file in an encrypted folder can be a temporary option, but it's not ideal for long-term storage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the "do not" list matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much. Do not paste the token into an unencrypted document that syncs to cloud storage. Do not email it to yourself. Do not save it in a public GitHub file. Treat it like a password.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then step three is using it when Git asks for credentials.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The next time Git prompts you over HTTPS, you enter your GitHub username in the username field, and paste the Personal Access Token into the password field.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Not your regular GitHub account password.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The token goes where Git says password.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the operating system may help remember it.

speaker: ALEX
text: On Windows, Git Credential Manager often stores it automatically after first use. On macOS, Keychain may offer to save it; the guidance here is to allow that, often by selecting Always Allow. On Linux, you may want to configure credential caching with a Git command so you're not prompted every single time.

speaker: ALEX
text: The command is: git config double-dash global credential dot helper cache.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So that's the recommended workshop path: generate, store securely, then use the token in place of a password when Git asks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's cover the alternative, because some learners will prefer SSH.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. The first step in SSH setup is checking whether you already have a key. In a terminal, run the command to list files in your.ssh directory.

speaker: ALEX
text: You're looking for names like id underscore rsa dot pub or id underscore ed25519 dot pub. If you see a matching private key and public key pair, you may already have a usable key.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If not, generate a new one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The command shown here uses the modern ed25519 algorithm with your email as a label. When prompted, press Enter to accept the default file location, and optionally set a passphrase. Optional in the sense that the command allows it, but recommended for security.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Git creates two files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The private key, usually named id underscore ed25519, must stay private. The public key, usually id underscore ed25519 dot pub, is the one you can share with GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That private-versus-public distinction is one of the most important concepts in this whole lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Never share the private key. Only upload the public key.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next comes copying the public key, and the commands vary by operating system.

speaker: ALEX
text: On Windows PowerShell, there's a command that reads the public key file and sends it to the clipboard. On macOS, pbcopy can copy the public key file directly into the clipboard. On Linux, the reference shows a command to print the file contents, then manually select and copy the output.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That manual-copy note deserves to be said out loud because it appears as a separate heading in the source.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good catch. On Linux in particular, if your setup doesn't provide a simple clipboard command, you may need to run the command that displays the key, then manually select and copy the full output yourself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once you've copied the public key, you add it to GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Go to github dot com slash settings slash keys. Choose New SSH key. Give it a descriptive title, like Workshop Laptop SSH Key. For key type, choose Authentication Key. Then paste the public key into the key field. It should begin with something like ssh dash ed25519 or ssh dash rsa. Then select Add SSH key and confirm with your password or two-factor authentication if prompted.

speaker: JAMIE
text: After that, test the connection.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Use the SSH test command to connect to GitHub. If everything is set up correctly, GitHub responds with a greeting like, "Hi username, you've successfully authenticated."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And from then on, you need to use SSH-style repository URLs, not HTTPS ones.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's crucial. The SSH format looks like git at github dot com, colon, owner slash repo dot git. The HTTPS format starts with https colon slash slash github dot com slash owner slash repo dot git. If you set up SSH but keep using an HTTPS remote, Git won't suddenly switch methods on its own.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That leads perfectly into switching between HTTPS and SSH.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. First, check your current remote with git remote dash v. That shows the fetch and push URLs currently configured for origin.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If you want SSH, set the origin URL to the SSH form.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Use git remote set-url origin followed by the SSH URL, like git at github dot com colon your-username slash repo dot git.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you want to go back to HTTPS, do the same with the HTTPS URL.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Same command pattern, different URL. This is useful if you started one way and later decided the other method fits your workflow better.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also accessibility guidance for troubleshooting here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For screen reader users, each troubleshooting entry starts with the error message itself, so jumping by heading is effective. The commands are in code blocks, so using a focused reading mode can help when copying. For low vision users, the error messages are intended to be easy to scan, and if terminal output is hard to read, it may help to paste commands into VS Code's integrated terminal where your own color theme and zoom settings apply. For sighted users, the practical advice is to locate the exact error heading and try the most common fix first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's go through the common failures.

speaker: ALEX
text: First: "Authentication failed" when pushing. If you're using HTTPS and a Personal Access Token, this usually means the token is expired, incorrect, or an old cached credential is interfering.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should the learner do?

speaker: ALEX
text: Generate a new PAT if needed. Then clear the cached credentials. On Windows that often means checking Credential Manager. On macOS, Keychain. On Linux, the command shown is git credential-cache exit. Then try pushing again so Git prompts you for fresh credentials.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Second: "Permission denied, publickey."

speaker: ALEX
text: That usually points to SSH. It means GitHub didn't accept the SSH key being offered, or no appropriate key was offered.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the fixes are?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, verify that your public key is actually added to your GitHub account at the SSH keys settings page. Then check whether the SSH agent is running and knows about your key. The reference suggests using ssh-add dash l to list loaded keys, and if necessary, adding your key with ssh-add followed by the path to your private key file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And third: "Host key verification failed."

speaker: ALEX
text: That one means your computer doesn't yet trust GitHub's host key, or the known-hosts record needs attention. The provided fix is to use ssh-keyscan github dot com and append the result to your known_hosts file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the theme in troubleshooting is: identify whether the problem is token-related, key-related, or host-trust-related.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's zoom out to security best practices, because authentication setup can accidentally turn into security mistakes if people rush.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. First, never share your private key or your Personal Access Token. Treat both like passwords. Second, use scoped PATs, meaning give them only the permissions you need. Third, set expiration dates on PATs so they don't live forever. Fourth, use a passphrase on SSH keys for an extra layer of protection. Fifth, revoke old tokens when you're finished with a project or stop using a device. And sixth, never commit tokens or keys into Git repositories. If a config file might contain secrets, make sure it's excluded appropriately, often with.gitignore.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That point about not committing secrets is huge in open source.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really is. Accidental secret exposure is one of the easiest mistakes for new contributors to make.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also introduces commit signing, verified badges, and something called vigilant mode. Even though the source excerpt is shortened, we should teach the concepts that are clearly signposted there.

speaker: ALEX
text: Agreed. Commit signing is related to authentication but serves a different purpose. Authentication proves you can access GitHub for operations like push. Commit signing proves that a particular commit was cryptographically signed by a key associated with you. On GitHub, signed commits may show a Verified badge, while unsigned or improperly configured commits may show as unverified.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So why does that matter?

speaker: ALEX
text: It adds trust and traceability. Maintainers and collaborators can see stronger evidence that a commit genuinely came from the claimed authoring identity and was not altered in transit. In some projects, especially security-conscious ones, signed commits are encouraged or even expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source mentions two methods for signing commits: SSH signing and GPG signing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. SSH signing is often the simpler option because it can reuse an SSH key you already have. GPG signing is the traditional method and has been common for years, but it usually involves more setup steps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source specifically highlights SSH signing as simpler because it reuses your existing SSH key.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and that's a practical recommendation for many learners. If you already set up SSH successfully, SSH signing may be the lowest-friction path to getting verified commit signatures.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But the material also includes a GPG path with some very specific prompts.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. If using GPG signing, one instruction is to choose RSA and RSA, 4096 bits, and never expires, and to enter your GitHub email address when prompted. Those details matter because the email on the key needs to align with your GitHub identity for GitHub to recognize the signature properly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's a step to find the key ID.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The output from GPG includes a line with something like sec rsa4096 slash followed by a long identifier. That identifier is the key ID. The source explicitly calls out that the Xs in that pattern stand in for your actual key ID.

speaker: JAMIE
text: After that, export the public key.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. You export the public portion of the GPG key and copy the block that begins with the line BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK. That public key block is what you add to GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then add it to GitHub, and configure Git to sign all commits.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Once GitHub has the corresponding public signing key, you can configure Git so new commits are signed automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's explain vigilant mode, because that term can sound intimidating.

speaker: ALEX
text: Vigilant mode is a GitHub feature some maintainers enable to emphasize commit signature verification more strongly. The reason some maintainers use it is simple: they want clearer security signals in repositories, especially for collaborative or sensitive projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does a contributor experience when vigilant mode is in play?

speaker: ALEX
text: You may notice that commit verification states become more noticeable, and unsigned commits may stand out more clearly. In practice, it means verification badges matter more in the social and review workflow. If you contribute to a repository with vigilant mode expectations, signing your commits can help avoid confusion and make your work easier to trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's an accessibility point here too: how to read verification badges with a screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The key idea is not to rely only on the visual badge. Screen reader users should inspect the accessible text associated with the verification state on the commit page or in commit details. In other words, listen for whether GitHub reports the commit as Verified or Unverified rather than assuming that a badge color or icon communicates enough.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So for this workshop, what's the practical recommendation?

speaker: ALEX
text: For authentication, the recommended approach is a Personal Access Token, because it's the most accessible and least disruptive setup path for many learners using HTTPS with Git. SSH remains a solid alternative if you're comfortable in the terminal and want a smoother long-term workflow. And for commit signing, if you decide to pursue verified commits later, SSH signing is often the simplest next step if you already use SSH, while GPG remains a traditional option with more setup overhead.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the appendix also points learners back to the bigger learning path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This material is a reference companion to pre-workshop setup and also connects to later practical Git work. It's not meant to be memorized all at once. It's a just-in-time guide for the moment authentication becomes necessary.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap up, can we give learners one short mental model to carry forward?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Here it is: if you use HTTPS, Git will usually ask for your username and a token. If you use SSH, Git will use your key pair. If something breaks, first identify which method you're using, then troubleshoot that method specifically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's clean. And the final reminder?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use official GitHub documentation as the authority when details change. Authentication interfaces evolve. Token pages move. Security defaults get updated. So use this lesson to understand the concepts and workflow, and use GitHub Docs to confirm the latest exact steps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Thanks for listening. If authentication has ever felt mysterious, hopefully now it feels more like a set of clear choices.

speaker: ALEX
text: And more importantly, choices you can make accessibly and securely.
